featuredSetups.description: Open a simple shear with the basis vectors and unit square visible.

setup.note: Use this to watch how changing where $e_2$ lands makes the whole plane lean right.

featuredSetups.description: Open a reflection and compare the original and transformed triangle.

setup.note: Use this to see that reflecting one basis direction flips the whole plane, not just one shape.

paragraphs: A 2 by 2 matrix is easiest to read as a rule that transforms the whole plane, not as a detached box of numbers. This bench keeps the grid, basis vectors, unit square, and sample triangle on one coordinate system so stretch, shear, reflection, and mixed actions stay visible together.

Read the columns first. The first column is $M e_1$ and the second column is $M e_2$. Once you know where those two basis vectors land, every other point follows by the same linear-combination rule, which is why the entire grid moves consistently.

keyIdeas: The two matrix columns tell you where $e_1$ and $e_2$ land, so they already predict the new grid and the image of the unit square.
Every vector is built from x copies of $e_1$ and y copies of $e_2$, so after transformation it becomes the same combination of $M e_1$ and $M e_2$.
Stretch, shear, and reflection are different plane-wide outcomes of the same linear rule.

commonMisconception.myth: Each entry moves the sample shape separately, so the grid and basis vectors are just extra decoration.
correction: The entries matter because they set the two basis-image columns.
Once $M e_1$ and $M e_2$ are fixed, the unit square, the grid, and every other point follow automatically from the same linear-combination rule.

workedExamples.title: Read the matrix from its columns
workedExamples.intro: Use one shear case and one reflection case. In each example, read the columns first, then check the same story on the plane.

items.title: Read the basis images in a shear
items.prompt: For the shear matrix $M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, where do $e_1$ and $e_2$ land, and what does that predict for the unit square?

steps.label: 2. Match the first column to $M e_1$
3. Match the second column to $M e_2$

applyAction.label: Show the shear
applyAction.observationHint: Watch $M e_2$ lean right while $M e_1$ stays on the x-axis. The unit square follows those two columns into a parallelogram.

items.title: Track one point through a reflection
items.prompt: Where does $p = \langle 1.6, 1.1 \rangle$ land under the reflection matrix $M = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, and what does that mean geometrically?

steps.label: 1. Write the rule for any point

applyAction.label: Show the reflection
applyAction.observationHint: The x-coordinate changes sign while the y-coordinate stays the same, so the point mirrors across the y-axis.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a matrix that shears the plane to the right without turning it into a reflection, and keep $M e_1$ close to the original x-axis direction.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before changing any entries, decide which column controls the right-leaning effect.
miniChallenge.answer: Keep $M e_1$ near $\langle 1, 0 angle$ and move $M e_2$ to the right, for example by making the second-column x-entry positive.
miniChallenge.explanation: A right shear comes from changing where $e_2$ lands while leaving $e_1$ almost unchanged. That tilts the unit square into a right-leaning parallelogram without flipping the plane.

equations.meaning: Records one linear rule that acts on every point in the plane.
The columns are the images of the two basis vectors, so they organize the transformed grid.
Any vector is rebuilt from the same basis-image columns.

variableLinks.description: Sets the horizontal part of $M e_1$.
Sets the vertical part of $M e_1$.
Sets the horizontal part of $M e_2$. Changing this is the quickest way to create a visible horizontal shear.
Sets the vertical part of $M e_2$.

controls.description: Move the image of $e_1$ left or right.
Move the image of $e_1$ up or down.
Move the image of $e_2$ left or right.
Move the image of $e_2$ up or down.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the original grid and shapes visible behind the transformed ones.
whatToNotice: You can compare the before and after on the same axes.
overlays.whyItMatters: A fixed reference makes stretch, shear, and reflection easier to read.

overlays.shortDescription: Show $e_1$, $e_2$, and their images under the matrix.
whatToNotice: The columns are the new basis directions for the transformed plane.
overlays.whyItMatters: Reading the basis images is the fastest route from matrix entries to geometry.

overlays.shortDescription: Track how the unit square becomes a parallelogram or flips orientation.
whatToNotice: The top-right corner lands at the sum of the two column vectors.
overlays.whyItMatters: The square gives one compact picture of the whole matrix action.

overlays.shortDescription: Show a non-square shape under the same transformation.
whatToNotice: The triangle changes by the same rule as the grid and the unit square.
overlays.whyItMatters: It reinforces that the matrix acts on every point, not just on basis vectors.

graphs.description: Blend from the identity matrix to the current matrix and track how one point's x- and y-coordinates change.
Blend from the identity matrix to the current matrix and compare how the lengths of the two basis images change.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the entries, the columns, and the plane picture connected. A good reading starts with the basis images and then checks the grid and shapes.

items.text: Change one column and watch the matching basis vector, the unit square, the grid, and the sample triangle all update together.
Increase the second-column x entry and watch $M e_2$ lean right while the grid lines stay straight. That is the signature of a shear.
Hover the blend graphs and compare the intermediate plane pictures. The transformation grows continuously from the identity matrix to the full matrix.

predictionMode.title: Predict the plane action
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the plane should do before you apply the new matrix.

items.prompt: If the second-column x entry $b$ becomes positive while the other entries stay at the identity values, what visible change should happen first?
items.explanation: A positive second-column x entry changes where $e_2$ lands, so the grid and the unit square shear to the right.
items.observationHint: Watch $M e_2$ first, then the square and the triangle.

items.prompt: If the first-column x entry becomes negative while the other entries stay near the identity, what should you expect?
items.explanation: Flipping the first basis direction across the y-axis reflects the plane in the x-direction.
items.observationHint: Compare the first basis vector and the mirrored triangle.

challengeMode.intro: Use the basis columns, the unit square, and the sample triangle together. The goal is to read one matrix as one plane-wide action, not as four separate numbers.

items.title: Build a right shear
items.prompt: Start from the identity matrix and create a right shear that keeps $M e_1$ close to the x-axis while the unit square becomes a right-leaning parallelogram.
items.successMessage: You built the shear by moving the second column, so the basis vectors, the square, and the triangle all lean together.

setup.note: Change the second column first. A right shear comes from moving where $e_2$ lands while leaving $e_1$ nearly unchanged.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the plane picture and the column rule together.

questions.explanation: The columns tell you where the two basis vectors land. From there, the grid, unit square, and every other vector follow.
Increasing the second-column x entry moves $M e_2$ to the right while keeping $M e_1$ near its original direction, which creates a right shear.
A reflection changes the whole linear action, so every point on the plane flips consistently, not just the sample triangle.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a coordinate plane with a transformed grid, two draggable basis-image vectors, a transformed unit square, a transformed sample triangle, and a tracked point. Sliders control the four matrix entries that determine where the two basis vectors land.
Changing an entry updates the basis images, the grid, the unit square, and the sample triangle together so the learner can connect the matrix columns to one plane-wide action.

One graph tracks the x- and y-coordinates of a fixed point while the current matrix is blended in from the identity matrix. The second graph tracks the lengths of the transformed basis vectors over the same blend.
Those graphs are tied to the same plane picture, so hovering them previews an intermediate transformation directly on the grid and shapes.
